mappings.rationaleAI: Movable objects supporting human activities correspond to objects used for interior decoration (C.3.c) and interior design as an art (C.3.a), spanning historical traditions from Egyptian joinery through 20th-century Modernism.
Clay-based decorative objects including earthenware, stoneware, and porcelain (B.1), with functional vessel intent placing pottery squarely within decorative arts classified by material.
Decorative worn items including rings, necklaces, bracelets, earrings, and brooches made from precious metals, gemstones, and enamel directly correspond to jewelry as functional design (C.1.b).
The intentional creation of plans balancing aesthetic and functional goals encompasses the nature and scope of decorative and functional design (A) and industrial design as an art (C.2).
Items worn on the human body made from textiles correspond to dress and dress accessories (C.1.a), functioning as both protection and social signaling.
Flowers and botanical materials used in bouquets, garlands, and wreaths (B.10.a) as decorative organic materials.
Fibre-based materials produced by weaving, knitting, and felting correspond to fabrics as decorative arts materials (B.2).
Glass as a decorative arts material (B.4) used in vessels, ornamental objects, and stained glass since Mesopotamian origins.
Paper as a decorative material (B.6), used in papier-mache, wallpaper, and other decorative applications.
Bronze as a decorative metal (B.5) used in architectural elements, ornamental objects, and coinage.
Gold as a precious decorative metal (B.5) and its use in coins, currency, and medals (C.5.a).
Copper as a decorative metal (B.5) with characteristic blue-green patinas used in architectural and ornamental applications.
The science of metals underpins the processing and properties of metals used in decorative arts (B.5).
Bone as a decorative organic material alongside shell, horn, and ivory (B.10.d).
Shoes as footwear items correspond to dress accessories (C.1.a), with fashion-oriented and culturally differentiated forms.
The broader visual arts context including the fine arts versus applied/decorative arts distinction provides the framework for understanding the nature and scope of decorative arts (A).
Stone as a decorative material (B.7) used in architectural ornamentation including rustication and serpentine masonry.
Natural rubber as a synthetic/composition material (B.9.b) used in functional design applications.
Iron as a decorative metal (B.5), historically significant in wrought-iron ornamental work.
